learning_objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of heart failure including the compensatory mechanisms and the targets for treatment.
Classify heart failure according to ejection fraction.
Identify the causes of heart failure.
Recognize the symptoms and signs of heart failure.
Outline the investigations to be performed to diagnose heart failure.
Outline the differential diagnosis of heart failure.
Summarize the pharmacological treatment and device therapy of heart failure and explain their rationale and the practical considerations with their use.
Recognize the acute heart failure presentation.
Identify the causes and precipitating factors of acute heart failure.
Outline the treatment of acute heart failure.
core_concepts: Classification of Heart Failure (acute vs chronic, right-sided vs left-sided, high output, NYHA functional classification, Ejection Fraction based classification)
Etiology of Heart Failure (diseases of myocardium, abnormal loading conditions, pericardial disease, arrhythmias)
Pathophysiology of HFrEF (reduced pumping capacity, reduced cardiac output, backward congestion, neurohormonal activation, LV remodeling)
Pathophysiology of HFpEF (impaired LV relaxation, reduced compliance, elevated intracardiac pressures, backward congestion)
Clinical Presentation of chronic Heart Failure (symptoms and signs related to left-sided congestion, right-sided congestion, and reduced cardiac output)
Investigations for Heart Failure (laboratory tests, ECG, CXR, echocardiography, further investigations based on etiology)
Treatment of HFrEF (non-pharmacological measures, pharmacological treatment including diuretics, ACEI/ARNI/ARB, beta-blockers, MRA, SGLT2 inhibitors, other drugs, and drugs to avoid)
Device Therapy for HFrEF (Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy - CRT, Implantable Cardiac Defibrillator - ICD)
Treatment of HFpEF (diuretics, SGLT2i, treatment of cause/comorbidities)
Acute Heart Failure (definition, etiology/precipitating factors, clinical presentation, clinical profiles like acute pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock, investigations, treatment)
prerequisite_concepts: Basic understanding of ischemic heart disease (IHD)
Basic understanding of valvular heart disease
Basic renal function and electrolyte balance
definitions: Heart failure (HF): A clinical syndrome characterized by typical symptoms (e.g. breathlessness, ankle swelling and fatigue) and signs (e.g. elevated jugular venous pressure, pulmonary crackles and peripheral oedema) caused by a structural and/or functional cardiac abnormality resulting in reduced cardiac output and/or elevated intracardiac pressures at rest or during stress.
NYHA functional classification Class I: No limitation of physical activity. Ordinary physical activity does not cause symptoms of HF.
NYHA functional classification Class II: Slight limitation of physical activity. Comfortable at rest, but ordinary physical activity results in symptoms of HF.
NYHA functional classification Class III: Marked limitation of physical activity. Comfortable at rest, but less than ordinary activity causes symptoms of HF.
NYHA functional classification Class IV: Unable to perform any physical activity without symptoms of HF, or symptoms of HF at rest.
Acute heart failure: Rapid onset or worsening of symptoms and/or signs of HF requiring urgent treatment and hospital admission.
Acute pulmonary edema: Acute decompensated heart failure with a more rapid onset and severe pulmonary congestion.
Cardiogenic shock: With signs of hypoperfusion: cold extremities, weak peripheral pulses, mental confusion, oliguria.

Advanced heart failure: Severe symptoms and/or signs of HF at rest (NYHA IV), recurrent hospitalizations despite guideline-directed medical therapy, requiring advanced therapies as mechanical circulatory support, palliative care or cardiac transplantation.
clinical_features: Chronic HF - Symptoms (dt left-sided congestion): dyspnea (severity from NYHA stage I to IV), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, cough (dry or with white frothy, sometimes blood-tinged, sputum, more at night).
Chronic HF - Symptoms (dt right-sided congestion): ankle swelling.
Chronic HF - Symptoms (dt reduced cardiac output): fatigue.
Chronic HF - Signs (General examination): tachycardia, elevated jugular venous pressure, bilateral pitting lower limb edema (sacral edema in bed-ridden patients).
Chronic HF - Signs (Cardiac examination): S3 ± S4 gallop (most specific sign), murmur.
Chronic HF - Signs (Respiratory examination): bilateral (basal) pulmonary crepitations.
Chronic HF - Signs (Abdominal examination): tender hepatomegaly.
Acute HF - Symptoms: acute dyspnea, orthopnea, PND, cough with frothy ± blood-tinged sputum.

Acute HF - Signs (General examination): Vital signs: tachycardia, tachypnea > 25/min, elevated jugular venous pressure, bilateral lower limb edema, cold extremities, weak peripheral pulses in shock state.
Acute HF - Signs (Cardiac examination): S3 ± S4 gallop, murmur.
Acute HF - Signs (Pulmonary): pulmonary crepitations (extent: initially basal, may extend to lung apices).
Acute HF - Initial examination should detect signs of respiratory or hemodynamic compromise (ABCDE approach to critically ill patient).
risk_factors: Diseases of the myocardium: IHD, inflammatory (post-myocarditis: infection (most commonly viral), immune-mediated), toxic (medications: antineoplastic drugs, recreational substance abuse: alcohol, cocaine, amphetamines), endocrinal (thyroid disease, adrenal disease, acromegaly, diabetes), nutritional (thiamine deficiency), electrolyte disturbance (hypocalcemia), infiltrative (amyloidosis), peripartum, stress-induced, genetic.
Abnormal loading conditions: HTN, valvular heart disease, congenital heart disease, high output states (severe anemia, sepsis, thyrotoxicosis), volume overload (renal failure, iatrogenic).

Acute HF causes/precipitating factors: Acute coronary syndrome, Hypertension, Arrhythmia (tachyarrhythmia or bradyarrhythmia), Acute mechanical cause (myocardial rupture complicating ACS, acute native or prosthetic valve regurgitation secondary to endocarditis), Pulmonary embolism, Infection (e.g. pneumonia, infective endocarditis, sepsis), Cardiac tamponade, Drugs (e.g. NSAIDs, corticosteroids, negative inotropic substances, cardiotoxic chemotherapeutics), Non-adherence with salt/fluid intake or medications, Toxic substances (alcohol, recreational drugs), Increased sympathetic drive, stress-related cardiomyopathy, Metabolic/hormonal derangements (e.g. thyroid dysfunction, diabetic ketosis, adrenal dysfunction), Severe anemia, Pregnancy and peripartum related abnormalities, Surgery and perioperative complications.
history_questions: Inquire about the presence and severity of dyspnea (at rest, on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea).
Ask about the presence and characteristics of cough (dry, frothy, blood-tinged, timing).
Assess for ankle swelling or other peripheral edema.
Question about fatigue and its impact on daily activities.
Elicit history of risk factors/etiologies such as IHD, HTN, diabetes, thyroid disease, substance abuse, previous cardiac events, or medication use.
physical_exam: General examination: Assess for tachycardia, elevated jugular venous pressure, bilateral pitting lower limb edema (sacral edema in bed-ridden patients). In acute HF, check vital signs for tachycardia, tachypnea (> 25/min), and signs of hypoperfusion (cold extremities, weak peripheral pulses in shock state).
Cardiac examination: Auscultate for S3 ± S4 gallop (most specific sign), and murmurs.
Respiratory examination: Auscultate for bilateral (basal) pulmonary crepitations (may extend to lung apices in acute HF).
Abdominal examination: Palpate for tender hepatomegaly.
In acute HF, perform an initial examination to detect signs of respiratory or hemodynamic compromise (ABCDE approach).
investigations: Laboratory tests: Complete blood count, serum sodium, serum potassium, serum urea, serum creatinine, liver function tests, fasting blood glucose, glycated hemoglobin, lipid profile, thyroid function tests, serum ferritin, iron, total iron binding capacity and transferrin saturation.

Natriuretic peptides (NP): Low levels can rule out the diagnosis of HF (high negative predictive value). Note: many cardiac and non-cardiac causes of elevated NP; levels can be disproportionately lower in obese patients.
ECG: To assess cardiac rhythm, rate, QRS morphology and duration and other abnormalities.
Chest X-ray (CXR): Can show cardiomegaly and pulmonary congestion as well as detect other potential causes of breathlessness.

Echocardiography: Key investigation to diagnose heart failure. Allows assessment of morphology and function of all cardiac structures (myocardium, valves, pericardium), including LVEF measurement and estimation of LV filling pressures (diastolic function).
Further investigations needed according to the etiology:
For coronary artery disease: coronary computed tomography angiography, stress testing, invasive coronary angiography.
For cardiomyopathies and myocarditis: cardiac magnetic resonance imaging, specific investigations for etiology.
For acute HF: Troponin, arterial blood gas analysis (in cases with respiratory distress or shock).
diagnosis: Heart failure is a clinical syndrome diagnosed based on typical symptoms and signs, caused by a structural and/or functional cardiac abnormality.
Echocardiography is the key investigation for diagnosis, allowing assessment of LVEF and diastolic function.
Low levels of Natriuretic Peptides (NP) can rule out the diagnosis of HF.
Classification by Ejection Fraction (HFrEF, HFmrEF, HFpEF) is essential for guiding diagnosis and management.
Differential diagnosis includes respiratory diseases, anemia, thyroid disease, liver disease, renal disease, obesity, deconditioning.
management: Treatment of HFrEF - Aims: improve symptoms, functional capacity, quality of life; reduce hospitalizations; reduce mortality.

Non-pharmacological measures (HFrEF): Patient education (disease, treatment, compliance, drugs to avoid like NSAIDs), lifestyle advice (salt restriction, adapted physical activity, smoking cessation), weight monitoring (twice weekly) and knowing when to contact healthcare professional, immunization (yearly influenza, pneumococcal disease).
Pharmacological treatment (HFrEF):
Diuretics (loop diuretics): To improve symptoms and signs of congestion.
Four classes of drugs for all patients (to improve symptoms, reduce hospitalizations and mortality):

1. Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEI) (e.g., ramipril, enalapril, captopril): Mechanism (arterial vasodilatation → ↓ afterload, anti-RAAS, anti-LV remodeling). Contraindications (bilateral renal artery stenosis, history of angioedema, pregnancy). Cautions (GFR < 30 ml/min/1.73m2, Cr > 2.5mg/dL, K > 5.0 mmol/L, SBP < 90mmHg). Titrated to maximum tolerated dose. Follow-up (blood pressure, renal functions).
Angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor (ARNI) (sacubtril/valsartan): Indicated as a replacement for ACEI if still symptomatic, or considered first line (especially in acute HF hospitalization).
Angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB): Indicated only if ACEI is not tolerated (ACEI-induced cough, history of angioedema and with caution in ACEI-induced angioedema).

2. Beta-blockers (BB) (e.g., bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, nebivolol): Mechanism (block sympathetic activity reducing heart rate and myocardial work). Contraindications (heart block, critical limb ischemia, asthma). Initiated in clinically stable euvolemic patients, slowly uptitrated. Follow-up (heart rate).
3. Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRA) (e.g., spironolactone, eplerenone): Mechanism (diuresis, anti-RAAS, anti-LV remodeling). Follow-up (renal functions).
4. SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., dapagliflozin, empagliflozin): Mechanism (natriuresis, osmotic diuresis, + not fully elucidated mechanism).
Other drugs: ivabradine (If channel inhibitor), digoxin, hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate.
Drugs to avoid: NSAID and cox-2 inhibitors, corticosteroids, non-dihydropyridine CCB (verapamil, diltiazem), thiazolidinediones (glitazones).
Device therapy (HFrEF):

Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT): Improves cardiac function by restoring synchrony of ventricular contraction. Indicated in symptomatic HF despite optimal medical therapy, in sinus rhythm, with LVEF ≤ 35% and a wide QRS (≥ 130 ms and LBBB or ≥ 150ms and non-LBBB).

Implantable cardiac defibrillator (ICD): Indicated for secondary prevention of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in patients recovered from ventricular arrhythmia causing hemodynamic instability, or primary prevention of SCD in symptomatic HF (NYHA class II - III), especially of an ischemic etiology (> 40 days post-MI), and an LVEF ≤ 35% despite ≥ 3 months of optimal medical therapy, provided expected to survive >1 year with good functional status.
Treatment of the cause, precipitating factors and co-morbidities (HFrEF).
Management of Advanced Heart Failure: Requires advanced therapies like mechanical circulatory support, palliative care, or cardiac transplantation.
Treatment of HFpEF:
Diuretics to relieve congestion.
SGLT2i: The only drug class shown to reduce mortality and morbidity in HFpEF.
Treatment of the cause, precipitating factors and co-morbidities (e.g., ACEI/ARB or MRA for hypertension, BB for atrial fibrillation or ischemic heart disease).
Treatment of Acute Heart Failure:
Oxygen and ventilatory support: Oxygen if SpO2 < 90% or PaO2 <60 mmHg, non-invasive positive pressure ventilation, intubation for progressive respiratory failure.
Treatment of the cause/precipitating factor.
According to the clinical profile (perfusion and congestion):
Congestion with adequate perfusion: IV diuresis (loop diuretic, dose guided by urine output), IV vasodilators (nitroglycerine) in acute pulmonary edema if SBP > 110mmHg.

Cardiogenic shock (hypoperfusion): Vasopressors (norepinephrine) ± inotropes (dobutamine). Short-term mechanical circulatory support (e.g., intra-aortic balloon pump, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation) can be considered as bridge therapy if no response to drugs.
Thromboprophylaxis.
Pre-discharge and long-term: Initiate and uptitrate evidence-based pharmacological therapy. Follow-up visit recommended 1-2 weeks after discharge.
complications: Advanced heart failure (severe symptoms, recurrent hospitalizations).
Cardiogenic shock (hypoperfusion, cold extremities, weak peripheral pulses, mental confusion, oliguria).
red_flags: Acute heart failure presentation (rapid onset or worsening of symptoms and/or signs requiring urgent treatment and hospital admission).
Signs of respiratory or hemodynamic compromise (e.g., tachypnea > 25/min, cold extremities, weak peripheral pulses, mental confusion, oliguria, requiring ABCDE approach).
Acute pulmonary edema (rapid onset, severe pulmonary congestion).
Progressive respiratory failure persisting despite oxygen administration or non-invasive ventilation (requires intubation).
Symptoms of HF at rest (NYHA Class IV).
must_not_miss_concepts: Heart failure is a clinical syndrome defined by symptoms, signs, and cardiac abnormality leading to reduced cardiac output and/or elevated intracardiac pressures.
Classification of HF by Ejection Fraction (HFrEF, HFmrEF, HFpEF) is critical for diagnosis and guiding management.
Echocardiography is the key diagnostic investigation for heart failure.
The four foundational pharmacological classes for HFrEF (ACEI/ARNI/ARB, Beta-blockers, MRA, SGLT2i) are essential for reducing mortality and hospitalizations.
SGLT2 inhibitors are the only drug class shown to reduce mortality and morbidity in HFpEF.
Recognition and urgent management of acute heart failure, including severe presentations like acute pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock.
Awareness of drugs that can worsen heart failure and should be avoided (NSAIDs, COX-2 inhibitors, corticosteroids, non-dihydropyridine CCB, thiazolidinediones).
Understanding the compensatory mechanisms in HFrEF (neurohormonal activation, LV remodeling) and how they become detrimental over time.
common_student_mistakes: Failing to recognize the specific symptoms and signs of heart failure.
Not classifying heart failure by ejection fraction, which is crucial for treatment decisions.
Overlooking non-pharmacological measures in the management of chronic HF.
Prescribing drugs that are contraindicated or can worsen heart failure (e.g., NSAIDs, non-dihydropyridine CCBs).
Not uptitrating guideline-directed medical therapy to maximum tolerated doses in HFrEF.
Delaying urgent treatment for acute heart failure, especially in severe presentations like cardiogenic shock or acute pulmonary edema.
Failing to identify and address precipitating factors in acute heart failure.
Not considering device therapy (CRT, ICD) in eligible patients with HFrEF.
Misinterpreting Natriuretic Peptide levels without considering confounding factors (e.g., obesity, renal disease).
clinical_reasoning_points: Differentiating between left-sided and right-sided congestion based on clinical features.

Understanding how the compensatory mechanisms in HFrEF (neurohormonal activation, LV remodeling) are initially beneficial but ultimately contribute to disease progression, providing the rationale for targeted therapies.
Interpreting Natriuretic Peptide levels in the context of patient comorbidities (e.g., renal disease, obesity) and other cardiac conditions.
Tailoring investigations based on the suspected etiology of heart failure.
Justifying the use of specific drug classes in HFrEF based on their mechanisms of action (e.g., ACEI for afterload reduction and anti-remodeling, beta-blockers for sympathetic blockade).
Applying practical considerations for drug use, including contraindications, cautions, titration strategies, and necessary follow-up monitoring (e.g., blood pressure, renal function, heart rate).
Using the NYHA functional classification to assess symptom severity and guide management decisions and prognosis.
Recognizing the different clinical profiles of acute HF (warm & dry, warm & wet, cold & wet, cold & dry) to guide immediate therapeutic interventions.
Prioritizing oxygen and ventilatory support in acute HF patients with respiratory distress.
Understanding the indications for device therapy (CRT, ICD) based on LVEF, QRS duration, and symptom status.
Emphasizing the importance of treating underlying causes and comorbidities in both chronic and acute heart failure.
assessment_opportunities: Case-based discussions on diagnosing and classifying heart failure based on clinical presentation and investigation results.
Interpretation of ECGs, chest X-rays, and echocardiography reports in patients with suspected or confirmed HF.
Formulating comprehensive management plans for patients with HFrEF and HFpEF, including non-pharmacological, pharmacological, and device therapies, with justification for choices.
Identifying appropriate follow-up monitoring parameters for patients on various HF medications.
Simulated patient encounters to practice recognizing and managing acute heart failure presentations, including identifying red flags and initiating emergency treatment.
Explaining the pathophysiology of HFrEF and HFpEF and how different treatments target these mechanisms.
Short answer questions or multiple-choice questions on definitions, etiologies, classifications, and mechanisms of action of key drugs in heart failure.
Clinical reasoning exercises involving differential diagnosis of breathlessness and edema in the context of heart failure.
high_yield_facts: Heart failure is a clinical syndrome characterized by typical symptoms and signs due to structural/functional cardiac abnormality leading to reduced cardiac output and/or elevated intracardiac pressures.
HF is classified by Ejection Fraction: HFrEF (<40%), HFmrEF (41-49%), HFpEF (≥50%).
S3 gallop is considered the most specific sign of chronic HF.
Echocardiography is the key diagnostic investigation for heart failure.
Low Natriuretic Peptide (NP) levels have a high negative predictive value and can rule out HF.
The four pillars of HFrEF pharmacological treatment (ACEI/ARNI/ARB, Beta-blockers, MRA, SGLT2i) are crucial for reducing mortality and hospitalizations.
SGLT2 inhibitors are the only drug class shown to reduce mortality and morbidity in HFpEF.
Drugs to avoid in HF include NSAIDs, COX-2 inhibitors, corticosteroids, non-dihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil, diltiazem), and thiazolidinediones.
Cardiac Resynchronization Therapy (CRT) is indicated in symptomatic HF patients with LVEF ≤ 35% and a wide QRS.
Implantable Cardiac Defibrillator (ICD) is indicated for primary and secondary prevention of sudden cardiac death in specific HF populations.
Acute heart failure is a medical emergency requiring urgent treatment and hospital admission.
Acute pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock are severe forms of acute HF requiring immediate intervention.
Management of acute HF is guided by the patient's perfusion and congestion status.
